core_behavior.note: Model B specifies a composable middleware/decorator pattern with jitter and thundering-herd concern; Model A and C describe simpler retry loops without architectural pattern requirements; all three omit specification of which failure types trigger retry and infinite loop risk.

inputs.note: Model A defaults max retries to 3 with 500ms initial delay; Model B defaults max_attempts to 3 with 200ms base delay and explicit retryable status codes (429,500,502,503,504); Model C defaults to 3 retries with 1 second delay, and the retry trigger conditions differ across models.

outputs.note: Model B specifies a rich structured RetryExhaustedError with attempt_log, elapsed time, and correlation ID; Models A and C specify only a basic error message or status on failure.

assumptions.note: Model A assumes 4xx codes are retryable while Model C explicitly assumes 4xx errors should not be retried; models also differ on default delay values and backoff specifics, and none address infinite retry loop risk.

failure_modes.note: Model B returns a structured RetryExhaustedError with full diagnostic context; Model A returns an HTTP status code and descriptive message; Model C returns an error message and logs details, with an additional case for invalid configuration halting retries.

edge_cases.note: Model C caps retry delay at 60 seconds; Model A caps at 30 seconds maximum retry delay; Model B uses max_delay_ms defaulting to 10,000ms; none address infinite retry loop risk as an edge case.

security_constraints.note: Model B has an empty security_constraints array while Models A and C both populate the field with different security concerns.

performance_constraints.note: Model B specifies concrete timeout parameters (per-attempt and total) with numeric defaults; Model A specifies latency thresholds and throughput targets; Model C provides only qualitative performance guidance.

summary: ASM (Assumption Divergence) was the most frequently assigned category, appearing in 3 of the 8 fields.
